heroOverview: Plasmacytosis is an abnormal increase in plasma cells — the antibody-secreting end-stage of B lymphocytes — either in the circulating blood (plasmacytaemia) or, more commonly, in the bone marrow, where a level exceeding 10% is the diagnostic threshold for plasma cell disorders. Plasma cells normally reside almost exclusively in lymphoid tissue and bone marrow, so their appearance in significant numbers in peripheral blood is always pathological. The finding spans a broad clinical spectrum from reactive plasmacytosis — triggered by viral infections, autoimmune disease, or vaccination — to malignant conditions including multiple myeloma and plasma cell leukaemia. Recognising the distinction is critical because reactive plasmacytosis is self-limiting while malignant plasmacytosis requires disease-modifying therapy and carries a guarded prognosis without treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Reactive plasmacytosis is common in any severe viral illness; plasma cell myeloma, the most serious form, has an age-adjusted incidence of approximately 7 per 100,000 per year in the United States
keyStats.demographics: Malignant plasmacytosis (myeloma) predominantly affects adults over 60; Black Americans have twice the incidence of white Americans for plasma cell neoplasms
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis for multiple myeloma is approximately 69 years; reactive plasmacytosis occurs at any age
keyStats.globalCases: Multiple myeloma accounts for approximately 176,000 new diagnoses worldwide annually (Global Cancer Observatory, 2020)

definition: Plasmacytosis (ICD-10: D72.822) refers to an increased proportion or absolute number of plasma cells in the bone marrow, peripheral blood, or both. In the bone marrow, plasma cells normally constitute fewer than 5% of nucleated cells; a level of 5-9% is considered borderline, 10-59% defines myeloma (in the appropriate clinical context), and 60% or more is the threshold for the WHO's plasma cell bone marrow criterion regardless of other features. Circulating plasma cells (plasmacytaemia) above 2,000/µL or comprising more than 20% of circulating white cells define plasma cell leukaemia, the most aggressive plasma cell neoplasm. Plasma cells are terminally differentiated B lymphocytes characterised by a clock-face nuclear chromatin pattern, eccentric nucleus, and abundant basophilic cytoplasm with a perinuclear hof reflecting the Golgi apparatus. When clonal, they secrete a monoclonal immunoglobulin (M-protein) detectable by serum protein electrophoresis. Reactive plasma cells are polyclonal and produce normal immunoglobulin; clonality is confirmed by immunohistochemistry or flow cytometry showing kappa or lambda light-chain restriction. The condition falls under haematology and, when malignant, medical oncology.

typesClassification.description: A polyclonal, transient rise in plasma cells in response to an infection (particularly Epstein-Barr virus, CMV, HIV, and rubella), autoimmune disease, drug reaction, or vaccination; resolves with the triggering condition.
An M-protein below 3 g/dL and bone marrow plasma cells below 10% without end-organ damage; a premalignant state that progresses to myeloma or related disorders at approximately 1% per year.
M-protein 3 g/dL or more, or bone marrow plasma cells 10-60%, without CRAB criteria (hypercalcaemia, renal failure, anaemia, bone lesions); intermediate progression risk.
Clonal bone marrow plasma cells above 10% with CRAB criteria or a myeloma-defining biomarker; the most common malignant plasma cell disorder.
Plasma cells above 20% of circulating leucocytes or an absolute count above 2,000/µL; the most aggressive form, with a median survival historically under one year.
A single clonal plasma cell mass in bone (osseous) or soft tissue (extraosseous) without systemic myeloma involvement.

primarySymptoms: Bone pain — particularly in the back, ribs, and hips — from plasma cell infiltration and cytokine-driven osteoclast activation causing lytic lesions and pathological fractures.
Persistent fatigue and exertional dyspnoea reflecting normochromic normocytic anaemia, the most common haematological complication of malignant plasmacytosis.
Recurrent bacterial infections (pneumonia, sinusitis, urinary tract infections) caused by immunoparesis — suppression of normal immunoglobulin production by the malignant clone.
Polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, confusion, and nausea from hypercalcaemia — released when osteoclasts resorb large volumes of bone.
Foamy urine, leg oedema, or elevated creatinine indicating light-chain-mediated renal tubular injury (cast nephropathy), a CRAB criterion for multiple myeloma.
Peripheral tingling, numbness, and weakness — peripheral neuropathy from light-chain deposition, amyloid formation, or treatment-related neurotoxicity.
Pathological fractures of vertebrae or long bones occurring with minimal or no trauma, caused by cortical bone destruction at lytic sites.
Hyperviscosity symptoms — blurred vision, headache, dizziness, and mucosal bleeding — most prominent in Waldenström macroglobulinaemia and in high-burden IgA or IgG3 myeloma.
Night sweats, unintentional weight loss, and fever as constitutional symptoms in aggressive or transformed plasma cell disease.
Spinal cord compression with acute back pain radiating to the legs, leg weakness, or loss of bladder and bowel control from vertebral collapse or extradural plasmacytoma.
earlyWarningSigns: Back pain that is disproportionate to activity level or radiates in a band-like pattern around the chest, suggesting vertebral involvement.
Recurrent infections with organisms such as Streptococcus pneumoniae or Haemophilus influenzae that healthy adults normally handle easily.
Unexplained fatigue alongside a mild normocytic anaemia on routine blood testing.
An incidentally detected M-protein on serum protein electrophoresis performed for any reason.
emergencySigns: Acute back pain with leg weakness, sensory level, or bladder incontinence — spinal cord compression requiring emergency imaging and steroids or surgery.
Acute confusion, drowsiness, or seizure with a corrected calcium above 3.5 mmol/L — severe hypercalcaemia.
Oliguria or sudden creatinine rise in a patient with known or suspected myeloma — acute cast nephropathy requiring urgent hydration and oncology input.
Hyperviscosity syndrome with visual loss, stroke-like symptoms, or haemorrhage — requires emergency plasmapheresis.
Pathological fracture with instability or cord compromise — emergency orthopaedic and radiotherapy assessment.

causes.description: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), cytomegalovirus (CMV), HIV, rubella, and measles trigger reactive B-cell proliferation and plasmacytic differentiation as part of the humoral immune response; the resulting plasmacytosis is polyclonal and temporary, resolving as the infection clears.

Systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease chronically stimulate the humoral immune system, producing persistent low-grade reactive plasmacytosis as B cells respond to sustained antigen presentation.

Chromosomal translocations — most commonly involving the immunoglobulin heavy chain locus at 14q32 and partners such as CCND1 (t(11;14)), FGFR3 (t(4;14)), or MAF (t(14;16)) — dysregulate cyclin expression and cell survival pathways, driving monoclonal plasma cell expansion from a single malignant precursor cell.

Certain medications (phenytoin, isoniazid, penicillin) and vaccination-related antigenic stimulation can transiently increase bone marrow plasma cells as part of a humoral immune activation; this reactive pattern is distinguished from malignancy by polyclonality and rapid resolution.

Nearly all cases of multiple myeloma are preceded by MGUS, which transforms to active myeloma at a rate of approximately 1% per year through accumulated secondary mutations, epigenetic changes, and bone marrow niche co-evolution that break immune surveillance.

A rare de-novo malignancy in which massively clonal plasma cells enter and proliferate in the blood, producing counts above 2,000/µL without a preceding chronic myeloma phase; associated with highly aggressive cytogenetics including deletion 17p and gain of 1q.

riskFactors.description: The incidence of multiple myeloma rises steeply with age, with median diagnosis at 69 years; the prevalence of MGUS also increases from approximately 2% at age 50 to over 5% in those over 70.

Black Americans have approximately twice the age-adjusted incidence of multiple myeloma compared to white Americans, with higher MGUS prevalence as a likely contributor; the underlying biological reason remains under investigation.

riskFactors.factor: Personal history of MGUS or smouldering myeloma
riskFactors.description: MGUS progresses to overt myeloma at 1% per year; higher-risk SMM has a 50% 5-year progression rate, making surveillance essential.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of plasma cell disorders
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of myeloma patients have approximately a 2-3-fold higher risk, suggesting shared genetic predispositions, although specific germline variants are not yet routinely tested.
Body mass index above 30 is associated with an approximately 20% higher relative risk of myeloma; adipose tissue-derived cytokines including IL-6 promote plasma cell survival.
Chronic exposure to benzene, pesticides, herbicides, and certain solvents is associated with elevated myeloma risk, likely through DNA damage to B-cell precursors.

Sustained immune activation from chronic infections, autoimmune disease, or prolonged inflammatory states may accumulate somatic mutations in plasma cell precursors, contributing to malignant transformation over time.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60 for malignant plasma cell disorders
Black Americans at twice the population rate for multiple myeloma
People with a prior MGUS diagnosis requiring annual surveillance
Patients with active viral infections (EBV, CMV, HIV) for reactive plasmacytosis
Workers with long-term occupational chemical or radiation exposure
diagnosisOverview: Evaluation begins when plasmacytosis is found on bone marrow biopsy performed for unexplained cytopenias, an M-protein on serum electrophoresis, suspicious back pain, or renal impairment. The diagnostic algorithm for clonal disease follows the IMWG (International Myeloma Working Group) 2014 criteria. Key initial investigations include serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) and immunofixation to detect and type the M-protein, serum free light-chain assay, 24-hour urine for Bence Jones protein, full blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel with calcium and creatinine, LDH, and beta-2 microglobulin. Bone marrow trephine biopsy with immunohistochemistry and flow cytometry confirms the plasma cell percentage, clonality by kappa/lambda ratio, and high-risk cytogenetics by FISH. Whole-body low-dose CT or PET-CT has replaced the conventional skeletal survey in most guidelines because it detects lytic lesions at smaller sizes. MRI of the whole spine is added when cord compression is suspected or when skeletal imaging is negative but myeloma biomarkers are positive. Reactive plasmacytosis is distinguished from clonal disease by polyclonality on immunofixation, absence of M-protein, and clinical context (recent viral illness, autoimmune flare, or drug exposure). The primary differentials are myeloma, MGUS, SMM, Waldenström macroglobulinaemia, lymphoma, and reactive states.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects and characterises the M-protein (monoclonal immunoglobulin) that is the hallmark of clonal plasma cell disorders.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw sent to a clinical biochemistry laboratory; results in 1-2 days. A characteristic 'spike' in the gamma region indicates an M-protein.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures kappa and lambda light chains in blood; an abnormal ratio indicates clonal plasma cell activity even in non-secretory myeloma where SPEP is negative.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 24-48 hours. Calculated kappa:lambda ratio above 100 or below 0.01 carries high diagnostic significance.

diagnosticTests.test: Bone marrow trephine biopsy with immunophenotyping
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies plasma cell percentage, confirms clonality by light-chain restriction, and provides cytogenetic material for FISH to identify high-risk translocations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A short procedure under local anaesthetic at the posterior iliac crest; mild soreness for 1-2 days. Results take 7-14 days for full cytogenetics.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies lytic bone lesions, pathological fractures, and soft-tissue plasmacytomas. Preferred over conventional skeletal surveys for sensitivity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Scan lasting 15-30 minutes; low radiation dose. PET-CT requires a radiotracer injection given 60 minutes before imaging.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies urinary Bence Jones protein (free light chains), a CRAB criterion for renal involvement and a prognostic marker.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: All urine collected over 24 hours in a large container; no blood draw required. Results in 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Key staging biomarkers in the ISS (International Staging System) for myeloma; elevated levels indicate higher disease burden and worse prognosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results typically same day.

treatmentOverview: Reactive plasmacytosis requires no specific treatment beyond addressing the underlying cause; the plasma cell elevation resolves spontaneously as the infection, drug reaction, or autoimmune flare remits. For clonal disease, management is risk-stratified by the IMWG and NCCN guidelines. MGUS requires no treatment but annual monitoring with serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP), free light chains, and full blood count, with escalation to biopsy if markers change. High-risk smouldering myeloma may be offered lenalidomide-based early intervention following ECOG E3A06 data demonstrating a delay in progression to symptomatic disease. Active multiple myeloma in patients eligible for autologous stem cell transplantation (ASCT) is treated with three to four cycles of induction quadruplet therapy — most commonly daratumumab, bortezomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone (Dara-VRd) — followed by high-dose melphalan conditioning and ASCT, then indefinite lenalidomide maintenance. Transplant-ineligible patients receive continuous Dara-VRd or modified VRd regimens. Relapsed or refractory myeloma is managed with successive lines of therapy: carfilzomib, pomalidomide, ixazomib, elotuzumab, isatuximab, selinexor, and belantamab mafodotin. BCMA-targeted CAR-T cell therapy (ciltacabtagene autoleucel, idecabtagene vicleucel) produces deep durable responses in heavily pre-treated patients. Bispecific antibodies (teclistamab, elranatamab) are approved for later lines. Supportive care — monthly zoledronic acid or denosumab to prevent skeletal events, aciclovir prophylaxis during bortezomib regimens, pneumococcal and influenza vaccination, and erythropoiesis-stimulating agents for treatment-related anaemia — is integral to every treatment plan.

medicalTreatments.name: Bortezomib-based triplet therapy (VRd or VCd)
medicalTreatments.description: The proteasome inhibitor bortezomib combined with immunomodulatory agents (lenalidomide or thalidomide) and dexamethasone forms the backbone of induction therapy for newly diagnosed myeloma. It induces unfolded protein stress in plasma cells, triggering apoptosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Overall response rates of approximately 80-90% in newly diagnosed myeloma; adding daratumumab to form a quadruplet raises response rates further.

medicalTreatments.name: Daratumumab (anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody)
medicalTreatments.description: A monoclonal antibody targeting CD38, a surface marker highly expressed on plasma cells; induces antibody-dependent cytotoxicity, phagocytosis, and complement activation. Used in front-line quadruplet regimens and as single agent or combination in relapsed disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adding daratumumab to VRd in newly diagnosed eligible patients improved progression-free survival by approximately 40% versus VRd alone in the GRIFFIN trial.

medicalTreatments.name: Autologous stem cell transplantation (ASCT)
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose melphalan chemotherapy followed by reinfusion of the patient's own haematopoietic stem cells; deepens response achieved by induction and extends progression-free survival by a median of approximately 12-18 months compared to induction alone in eligible patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the DETERMINATION trial, ASCT after VRd prolonged median progression-free survival by 18 months (67 vs 46 months) versus VRd alone without transplant.

medicalTreatments.name: Lenalidomide maintenance
medicalTreatments.description: Oral immunomodulatory drug continued indefinitely after ASCT; reduces myeloma cell proliferation and enhances T-cell and NK-cell immunity against residual disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Lenalidomide maintenance after ASCT extends overall survival by approximately 2-3 years compared to placebo in randomised trials (IFM 2005-02, CALGB 100104).

medicalTreatments.name: BCMA-targeted CAR-T cell therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Autologous T cells engineered to target B-cell maturation antigen (BCMA) on myeloma cells; produces deep, durable responses in patients who have received at least three prior lines of therapy including an immunomodulatory agent, a proteasome inhibitor, and an anti-CD38 antibody.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Ciltacabtagene autoleucel achieved an overall response rate of 98% and a 12-month progression-free survival of approximately 77% in the CARTITUDE-1 trial.

surgicalOptions.name: Kyphoplasty or vertebroplasty
surgicalOptions.description: Minimally invasive procedures to stabilise painful vertebral compression fractures by injecting bone cement (polymethylmethacrylate) into the collapsed vertebra, with kyphoplasty additionally using a balloon to restore vertebral height.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 80-90% achieve significant pain reduction; height restoration is partial in most cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical decompression and stabilisation for spinal cord compression
surgicalOptions.description: Emergency or urgent surgery to relieve spinal cord or nerve root compression from vertebral collapse or extradural plasmacytoma, typically followed by adjuvant radiotherapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Neurological recovery depends heavily on pre-operative deficits; surgery achieves decompression in most cases, with roughly 50-70% showing functional neurological improvement.

alternativeTreatments.name: Bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid or denosumab)
alternativeTreatments.description: Standard supportive therapy for myeloma bone disease; reduce skeletal-related events (fractures, spinal cord compression, hypercalcaemia) by inhibiting osteoclast activity.

alternativeTreatments.name: Physiotherapy and rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured exercise and physiotherapy after vertebral fracture, kyphoplasty, or spinal surgery preserve function, reduce pain, and counter corticosteroid-related myopathy.

whySeeSpecialist: A haematologist or haematological oncologist should be seen without delay when bone marrow plasma cells exceed 10%, when an M-protein is detected on protein electrophoresis, or when any CRAB criteria are present. MGUS requires at minimum annual haematology review because the risk of malignant transformation persists lifelong. Spinal cord compression, severe hypercalcaemia, or acute renal failure require emergency specialist input. Early specialist involvement allows access to clinical trials and protects patients from the irreversible end-organ damage that can result from delayed myeloma diagnosis.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a haematologist or haematological oncologist at a centre with a dedicated myeloma programme, ideally one that participates in clinical trials and offers BCMA-targeted CAR-T cell therapy and ASCT. Academic medical centres provide access to next-generation cytogenetic testing, novel investigational agents, and multidisciplinary bone, renal, and neurology input. For MGUS, most haematology practices can provide guideline-appropriate annual surveillance; referral to a myeloma specialist is warranted when risk markers suggest high progression risk.
preventionStrategies: There is no established strategy to prevent MGUS or multiple myeloma, but maintaining a healthy weight reduces the IL-6-driven plasma cell survival associated with obesity.
Minimise occupational exposure to benzene, pesticides, and industrial solvents where possible.
People diagnosed with MGUS should attend scheduled annual monitoring to detect progression at an early, more treatable stage.
Vaccination against pneumococcus, meningococcus, and seasonal influenza reduces the risk of infectious complications in immunoparesis.
Avoid corticosteroids and nephrotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, iodinated contrast) without specialist guidance in known myeloma to protect kidneys.
lifestyleModifications: Stay well hydrated — at least 2-3 litres of fluid daily — to protect kidneys from light-chain cast nephropathy.
Engage in weight-bearing exercise as tolerated; discuss safe activities with your haematologist because bone fragility requires modification of high-impact sport.
Eat a balanced diet adequate in protein and calcium; vitamin D supplementation is often recommended given corticosteroid use and potential bone disease.
Avoid NSAIDs and nephrotoxic over-the-counter analgesics; use paracetamol for mild pain instead.
Report any new back pain, limb weakness, or bladder and bowel changes to your team immediately — these may signal cord compression.

recommended: Adequate fluid intake (2-3 litres daily) to flush light chains through the kidneys
Calcium and vitamin D-rich foods or supplements to counter corticosteroid-related bone loss
High-protein diet to counter cachexia and treatment-related weight loss
Small, frequent meals if nausea from chemotherapy or hypercalcaemia reduces appetite
avoid: Excessive calcium supplementation beyond guideline recommendations in hypercalcaemia episodes
Grapefruit and pomelo, which inhibit CYP3A4 and can alter blood levels of lenalidomide and other agents
High-phosphate processed foods that stress kidneys already affected by cast nephropathy
Alcohol, which worsens peripheral neuropathy from bortezomib and thalidomide

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is strongly encouraged throughout myeloma treatment to counter fatigue, corticosteroid myopathy, and deconditioning. Walking, swimming, and low-impact aerobics are appropriate. High-impact activities (running, contact sports, heavy weightlifting) should be avoided if bone lesions are present until structural integrity is confirmed by imaging. ASCT patients typically resume light activity within 4-6 weeks of infusion. All exercise programmes should be discussed with the treating haematologist to account for bone status and current treatment side effects.
dailyManagement: Take all prescribed medications at the times recommended; steroids are typically given on specific days of each cycle, not daily.
Monitor for signs of infection — fever above 38°C in a neutropenic patient warrants same-day medical review.
Keep a pain diary and report any sudden worsening of back pain immediately.
Attend all scheduled blood count checks; neutropenia and thrombocytopenia often require dose adjustments.
Use prescribed antiviral prophylaxis (aciclovir or valaciclovir) throughout bortezomib-containing regimens to prevent herpes zoster reactivation.
prognosis: The prognosis for plasmacytosis varies enormously by aetiology. Reactive plasmacytosis has an excellent outlook: it resolves completely once the underlying infection, drug reaction, or autoimmune episode is addressed. Multiple myeloma remains incurable with current standard therapy in the large majority of patients, but outcomes have improved dramatically over two decades. Median overall survival has risen from approximately 3 years in the early 2000s to over 6-8 years in modern triplet and quadruplet series, with some high-risk groups still below this and standard-risk patients achieving 10-year survival rates above 30% in registry data. High-risk cytogenetics (deletion 17p, t(4;14), t(14;16), gain 1q) are associated with shorter remission durations. Plasma cell leukaemia retains a median survival of under 2 years even with novel agents. CAR-T cell therapy and bispecific antibodies are redefining the treatment landscape for relapsed disease, with deep durable responses that were previously unattainable.
recoveryTimeline: Reactive plasmacytosis resolves within 2-6 weeks of treating the viral or inflammatory trigger. Myeloma induction therapy typically lasts 4-6 months; ASCT recovery requires 2-3 months before counts and energy normalise. Lenalidomide maintenance is taken indefinitely or until disease progression. Patients on continuous oral regimens may achieve stable disease control allowing near-normal daily function for years between active treatment episodes.
complications: Skeletal-related events — pathological fractures, vertebral collapse, and spinal cord compression from osteolytic bone destruction; prevented with bisphosphonates and radiotherapy.
Renal failure — cast nephropathy from light-chain precipitation in renal tubules can cause acute or chronic kidney disease requiring dialysis in a minority.
Severe infections — immunoparesis from myeloma and treatment-related immunosuppression cause life-threatening pneumococcal pneumonia, herpes zoster, and opportunistic infections.
Hypercalcaemia — bone resorption releases calcium at rates that can cause cardiac arrhythmia, confusion, and renal failure if not urgently managed.
Hyperviscosity syndrome — high M-protein levels thicken blood, impairing microcirculation; causes visual loss, stroke, and mucosal haemorrhage.

Treatment-related complications — peripheral neuropathy from bortezomib or thalidomide, venous thromboembolism from immunomodulatory agents, and secondary malignancy from alkylating agents and lenalidomide.

supportResources.name: International Myeloma Foundation (IMF)
supportResources.description: Leading global patient organisation for multiple myeloma with educational resources, a nurse helpline, clinical trial finder, and support groups.

supportResources.name: Myeloma UK
supportResources.description: UK charity providing patient information, a nurse helpline, and advocacy for myeloma patients and their carers.

supportResources.name: NIH National Cancer Institute — Plasma Cell Neoplasms
supportResources.description: Authoritative information on multiple myeloma, diagnosis, staging, and treatment from the US National Cancer Institute.

sources.title: Rajkumar SV et al. International Myeloma Working Group updated criteria for the diagnosis of multiple myeloma. Lancet Oncology, 2014
Palumbo A, Anderson K. Multiple myeloma. New England Journal of Medicine, 2011
Moreau P et al. Multiple myeloma: ESMO Clinical Practice Guidelines, 2021
Munshi NC et al. Idecabtagene vicleucel in relapsed and refractory multiple myeloma. NEJM, 2021
Kyle RA et al. Prevalence of monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance. NEJM, 2006
IMF — International Myeloma Foundation Patient Handbook
WHO Classification of Haematopoietic and Lymphoid Tumours, 5th Edition, 2022

confusedWithConditions.name: Waldenström macroglobulinaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Waldenström disease involves a lymphoplasmacytic clone that secretes IgM; unlike myeloma it rarely causes lytic bone lesions but frequently causes hyperviscosity. Both produce an M-protein but have different treatment algorithms.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leukaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leukaemia involves malignant expansion of myeloid or lymphoid blast cells, not plasma cells; plasma cell leukaemia is a distinct entity but can resemble acute leukaemia on a blood film and requires immunophenotyping to differentiate.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lymphoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lymphoma arises from lymphocytes that have not terminally differentiated to plasma cells; distinguishing lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma from myeloma requires marrow biopsy with immunophenotyping and cytogenetics.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple myeloma
relatedConditions.relevance: Multiple myeloma is defined by malignant plasmacytosis exceeding 10% in the bone marrow with end-organ damage; plasmacytosis is the pathological hallmark of the disease.

relatedConditions.name: Waldenström macroglobulinaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: A closely related lymphoplasmacytic neoplasm that causes IgM M-protein and bone marrow infiltration by plasma cells and lymphoplasmacytic cells; part of the differential in any patient with marrow plasmacytosis.

relatedConditions.name: Anaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Normocytic anaemia is the most common haematological finding in myeloma, caused by marrow infiltration, EPO suppression by cytokines, and renal failure.

relatedConditions.name: Polycythaemia vera
relatedConditions.relevance: A myeloproliferative neoplasm that — like clonal plasmacytosis — arises from a haematopoietic stem cell mutation; both are managed by haematologists with overlapping diagnostic infrastructure.

faqs.question: What is plasmacytosis?
faqs.answer: Plasmacytosis means there are too many plasma cells — the antibody-making white blood cells — in the bone marrow or blood. It ranges from a harmless reactive increase during a viral illness to a sign of serious conditions such as multiple myeloma.

faqs.question: What causes plasmacytosis?
faqs.answer: Common causes include viral infections such as EBV and CMV, autoimmune diseases, drug reactions, and the premalignant condition MGUS. Multiple myeloma is the most serious cause and involves a malignant clone of plasma cells in the bone marrow.

faqs.question: Is plasmacytosis the same as multiple myeloma?
faqs.answer: Not always. Plasmacytosis describes elevated plasma cells and can be reactive and benign. Multiple myeloma is a specific diagnosis requiring clonal plasma cells above 10% in the marrow plus either organ damage or myeloma-defining biomarkers. Not all plasmacytosis is myeloma.

faqs.question: What percentage of plasma cells in the bone marrow is abnormal?
faqs.answer: Normal bone marrow has fewer than 5% plasma cells. A level of 10% or more in the right clinical context meets one criterion for multiple myeloma. Smouldering myeloma uses a threshold of 10-60%, and plasma cell leukaemia requires more than 20% of circulating white cells to be plasma cells.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of plasmacytosis or myeloma?
faqs.answer: Bone pain (especially back and ribs), persistent fatigue, recurrent infections, thirst and confusion from high calcium, foamy urine from kidney involvement, and numbness in the hands and feet are the classic features. Reactive plasmacytosis usually has symptoms of the underlying infection rather than its own distinct features.

faqs.question: How is plasmacytosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis typically involves serum protein electrophoresis to detect an M-protein, a serum free light-chain test, and a bone marrow biopsy with immunophenotyping. Whole-body CT or PET-CT detects bone lesions. The IMWG criteria are used to classify findings into MGUS, smouldering myeloma, or active myeloma.

faqs.question: What is MGUS and is it dangerous?
faqs.answer: MGUS (monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance) is a premalignant state with a small M-protein and fewer than 10% marrow plasma cells and no organ damage. It is not cancer, but it progresses to myeloma at about 1% per year, so annual monitoring is recommended.

faqs.question: Can plasmacytosis be cured?
faqs.answer: Reactive plasmacytosis resolves fully when the underlying cause is treated. Multiple myeloma is currently incurable in most patients with standard therapy, though outcomes have improved greatly and some patients with standard-risk disease achieve very long remissions exceeding 10 years.

faqs.question: How is myeloma treated?
faqs.answer: Newly diagnosed myeloma is treated with induction therapy — typically a four-drug regimen including daratumumab, bortezomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone — followed by stem cell transplant in eligible patients and then lenalidomide maintenance. CAR-T cell therapy is available for relapsed disease.

faqs.question: Does plasmacytosis cause bone pain?
faqs.answer: In multiple myeloma, malignant plasma cells stimulate osteoclasts to resorb bone, creating lytic lesions that cause deep bone pain — most often in the back, ribs, and pelvis — and raise the risk of pathological fractures. Reactive plasmacytosis does not cause bone pain.

faqs.question: What is M-protein and what does it mean?
faqs.answer: M-protein (monoclonal protein) is an identical immunoglobulin made by a single abnormal plasma cell clone. Its presence on a blood or urine test is a marker of a clonal plasma cell disorder. Small M-proteins occur in MGUS; larger amounts suggest smouldering or active myeloma.

faqs.question: Can reactive plasmacytosis turn into myeloma?
faqs.answer: Reactive plasmacytosis — driven by a viral infection, drug, or inflammatory disease — is polyclonal and does not transform into myeloma. MGUS, however, which is a clonal (monoclonal) low-level disorder, does carry a 1% per year risk of progression to active myeloma.

faqs.question: What are the kidneys at risk of in myeloma?
faqs.answer: Light chains secreted by myeloma cells precipitate in the kidney tubules forming casts, a condition called cast nephropathy or myeloma kidney. This is the most common cause of renal failure in myeloma and can be partly reversed with rapid reduction of the light-chain burden using chemotherapy and vigorous hydration.

faqs.question: What is a plasma cell?
faqs.answer: A plasma cell is the final stage of B-lymphocyte development, specialised for producing large quantities of one specific antibody. Normal plasma cells live in lymph nodes and bone marrow. When a plasma cell becomes malignant, it proliferates uncontrollably and fills the marrow, crowding out normal blood cell production.

faqs.question: Is plasmacytosis found on a routine blood test?
faqs.answer: Reactive plasmacytosis can appear as an elevated lymphocyte count with atypical cells on a full blood count differential. An M-protein — the clue to clonal disease — is found on serum protein electrophoresis, not a standard blood count. Many myeloma cases are first suspected from abnormal routine tests.

faqs.question: How serious is plasma cell leukaemia?
faqs.answer: Plasma cell leukaemia is the most aggressive plasma cell disorder, with malignant plasma cells flooding the bloodstream. Even with modern therapy including bortezomib, daratumumab, and CAR-T cell approaches, median survival remains under two years for most patients, though outcomes are improving.

faqs.question: What is a plasmacytoma?
faqs.answer: A plasmacytoma is a single tumour mass of clonal plasma cells, either in bone or in soft tissue, without evidence of systemic myeloma. It is treated with radiotherapy and carries a better prognosis than myeloma, though up to 50-70% eventually progress to systemic disease.

faqs.question: Can myeloma affect the spine?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vertebral lytic lesions can collapse the vertebral bodies, causing severe back pain, height loss, and kyphosis. If the collapse impinges on the spinal cord or nerve roots it causes cord compression, a haematological emergency needing urgent steroids, radiotherapy, or surgery.

faqs.question: Do I need a bone marrow biopsy for plasmacytosis?
faqs.answer: A bone marrow biopsy is required to definitively quantify plasma cells, confirm clonality, and classify the condition. It is performed when M-protein is detected, when the free light-chain ratio is abnormal, when suspicious symptoms are present, or when MGUS monitoring suggests progression.

faqs.question: What follow-up is needed after an MGUS diagnosis?
faqs.answer: MGUS requires monitoring every 6-12 months with serum protein electrophoresis, free light-chain assay, and full blood count. Higher-risk MGUS — larger M-protein, abnormal free light-chain ratio, or non-IgG subtype — needs more frequent review. Most MGUS patients live normal lives and never develop myeloma.

metaDescription: Plasmacytosis means too many plasma cells in blood or marrow, from viral illness to myeloma. Learn causes, diagnosis, and treatments. Doctor-reviewed.
